planting.note: Plant fresh seeds or divisions in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained woodland soil in full shade to partial shade; giant blue cohosh blooms before the leaves are fully expanded (like trilliums and other spring woodland ephemerals), requiring rich, moist, undisturbed woodland conditions. Seeds require double dormancy — germination may take two seasons.

directions: Plant divisions in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained woodland soil in deep to partial shade; giant blue cohosh is the larger of the two North American blue cohosh species, blooming slightly earlier in spring than common blue cohosh (C. thalictroides) and producing larger, more dramatic purplish-bronze flowers before the leaves expand — giving a ghostly, early-spring display in rich maple-basswood and beech-maple forest understories. The glaucous blue-green, compound foliage that follows is attractive throughout summer, and the brilliant blue, berry-like seeds (technically a fleshy seed coat — not a true berry) are ornamental in late summer. Like common blue cohosh, this species is toxic and has a history of dangerous misuse as an abortifacient. A beautiful native for deep woodland shade gardens with consistently moist, rich soil — particularly under large maples, beeches, or basswood where it grows naturally.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall for natural cold stratification and spring germination in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to lean, rocky or sandy soil in partial shade; Fendler's meadow rue grows naturally in open to lightly shaded canyon slopes, aspen groves, and montane meadow edges of the southern Rockies and Great Basin.

directions: Direct sow in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to lean, rocky or sandy soil in partial shade; Fendler's meadow rue is the native meadow rue of the southern Rocky Mountains and Great Basin, growing in aspen groves, canyon-side scrub, and montane meadow margins. Like other meadow rues, it produces elegant, compound, blue-green, maidenhair-fern-like foliage — the primary ornamental feature — and loose panicles of small, wind-pollinated flowers. Fendler's meadow rue is dioecious (separate male and female plants), with the male plants having the showier flowers due to the dangling, yellow-green stamens. Notably more drought-tolerant than its eastern relatives, adapted to the drier conditions of the Rocky Mountain West. The finely textured, glaucous foliage provides excellent texture contrast in dry, shaded or dappled garden sites. A useful native for rock gardens, aspen-garden companion plantings, and dry shade under western native trees.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral woodland soil in shade to partial shade; yellow fumewort self-sows freely in moist woodland understories, appearing as tiny ferny seedlings in early spring and completing its cycle by late spring before summer heat. A true spring ephemeral annual.

directions: Direct sow in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, rich woodland soil in full shade to partial shade; yellow fumewort is a small, delicate woodland annual of rich, moist floodplain forests and mesic slope forests of the eastern US, producing finely dissected, ferny, pale blue-green foliage and tiny clusters of small, pale yellow, spurred flowers in early spring. Despite the modest size and inconspicuous flowers, yellow fumewort makes a charming and understated element in woodland gardens, filling in the bare ground under deciduous trees in early spring when little else is growing. Self-sows reliably in appropriate moist, shaded woodland soil to naturalize and expand each year. The plant is gone entirely by early summer, leaving no trace — true spring ephemeral behavior. Often found growing alongside Dutchman's breeches, spring beauty, trout lily, and other spring ephemerals in rich eastern floodplain forest. A specialist plant for authentic native woodland garden compositions.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, average to lean woodland or disturbed soil in partial shade to shade; pale corydalis is a weedy annual that germinates in fall or early spring and grows in disturbed woodland edges, open woodlands, and roadsides across the eastern US. Self-sows prolifically.

directions: Direct sow in fall in moist to average, slightly acidic to neutral, average to lean woodland, edge, or disturbed soil in partial shade; pale corydalis is a widespread eastern US native annual or biennial, smaller and more adaptable than yellow fumewort, growing in a wider range of disturbed, edge, and open woodland habitats from the Midwest south and east to the Atlantic Coastal Plain. The pale cream to yellow, small, spurred flowers are typical of the Corydalis genus. It is more tolerant of dry conditions and disturbed soils than yellow fumewort, commonly appearing as a winter annual in disturbed sites, roadsides, and the edges of open woods across its range. The lacy, fernlike foliage is consistently attractive. An easy, self-sowing, low-maintenance annual for naturalizing in lightly shaded gardens, woodland edges, and naturalistic settings where the delicate ferny texture provides spring interest before disappearing in early summer.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in fall or sow fresh seeds in fall in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained woodland soil in full shade to partial shade; slender toothwort is a spring ephemeral of rich, moist, mesic slopes and ravine forests of the eastern US, disappearing completely by early summer.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in fall in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained woodland soil in full shade to partial shade; slender toothwort is a charming native spring ephemeral of the eastern US, producing narrow, simple or lightly divided leaves (narrower than the more familiar cutleaf toothwort) and clusters of small, white to pale pink four-petaled flowers in early spring — one of the first native wildflowers to bloom in the woodland understory. The plant grows from a toothed, horizontal rhizome (the 'tooth' of the common name) that historically was chewed for a peppery, horseradish-like flavor. Goes completely dormant by late spring. Naturalizes by spreading rhizomes and self-sowing to form loose drifts in appropriate conditions. Best grown in combination with other spring ephemerals — trillium, trout lily, spring beauty — in rich, moist shade gardens. An easy and rewarding native for authentic woodland garden underplantings.
